channel.title: Clusters on Dijure | Digital Architectures with a hint of Java
channel.description: Recent content in Clusters on Dijure | Digital Architectures with a hint of Java
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 by Dijure LLC · All rights reserved · Some design elements by Themefisher. Select images from Unsplash. Created using Hugo.

item.title: Experience First Cluster
item.description:
This introduces you to labs that host a small Kubernetes cluster for your learning and experimentation. The Kubernetes cluster is small and temporary but will give you access to the majority of features you would find in any Kubernetes cluster.

In this lab, you will learn how to:

    ☐ Use the kubectl command-line tool

    ☐ Inspect the health of a running cluster

    ☐ Access the administration dashboard

    ☐ Create, update, read and delete (CRUD) a basic Kubernetes resource

item.title: K3s, Lightweight Kubernetes
item.description:
In this lab you’ll learn how to bootstrap a Kubernetes cluster using K3s.

For developers and operations, a powerful time-saver is to use the same common techniques and tooling wherever Kubernetes runs. Kubernetes installations are often targeted for vast collections of generic VMs with a commercial cloud service. There is also a large community of IoT and edge computing engineers targeting Kubernetes to eclectic hardware.

Rancher has developed K3s , a flavor of Kubernetes intentionally designed to be lightweight to target hardware with constrained resources and spartan services. It’s easy to install, fast to configure, and has a small memory footprint, all in a binary less than 55 megabytes.

item.title: kubeadm Quickstart
item.description:
In this lab, you’ll learn how to bootstrap a Kubernetes cluster using kubeadm.

kubeadm is a tool built to provide best-practice “fast paths” for creating Kubernetes clusters. It performs the actions necessary to get a minimally viable, secure cluster up and running in a user-friendly way. kubeadm’s scope is limited to the local node filesystem and the Kubernetes API, and it is intended to be a composable building block of higher-level tools.

item.title: KubeKey Quickstart
item.description:
In this lab, you’ll learn how to bootstrap a Kubernetes cluster using KubeKey.

For developers and operations, a powerful time-saver is to use the same common techniques and tooling wherever Kubernetes runs. Often Kubernetes installations are targeted to vast collections of generic virtual machines (VMs) with a commercial cloud service. There is also a community of IoT and edge-computing engineers targeting Kubernetes to more eclectic hardware.

KubeKey represents an installation tool to substitute for Ansible-based installers. KubeKey provides flexible installation choices, as you can install Kubernetes only or install both Kubernetes and KubeSphere. KubeSphere provides a collection of helpful add-ons tuned for the different varieties of Kubernetes clusters.

item.title: Kubernetes in Docker (kind)
item.description:
For developers and operations, a powerful time-saver is to use the same common techniques and tooling wherever Kubernetes runs. Often Kubernetes installations are targeted for vast collections of generic virtual machines (VMs) with a commercial cloud service. However, small clusters that can run right on your laptop—yet that offer most of the same features of larger Kubernetes services (e.g., Kubernetes-as-a-Service, or KaaS)—break down the barriers to mastering Kubernetes.

One approach to running a Kubernetes cluster uses Docker containers to simulate multiple Kubernetes nodes instead of running everything in a VM. The kind project provides a great experience for launching and managing test clusters in Docker. kind stands for Kubernetes in Docker. kind is a work in progress (pre-1.0) but is widely used by those building Kubernetes for fast and easy testing.

item.title: MicroK8s Quickstart
item.description:
In this lab, you’ll learn how to bootstrap a Kubernetes cluster using MicroK8s.

MicroK8s is a great way to run and familiarize yourself with Kubernetes. It’s single-package, fully conformant, lightweight Kubernetes that works on 42 flavors of Linux, perfect for:

  • Developer workstations
  • IoT
  • Edge computing
  • Rapid testing, experimentation, and learning

Why MicroK8s? Because it’s:

  • Small: Developers want the smallest K8s for laptop and workstation development. MicroK8s provides a standalone K8s compatible with Azure Kubernetes Service, Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service, and Google Kubernetes Engine when you run it on Ubuntu.
  • Simple: Minimize administration and operations with a single-package install that has no moving parts, for simplicity and certainty. All dependencies and batteries included.
  • Secure: Updates are available for all security issues and can be applied immediately or scheduled to suit your maintenance cycle.
  • Current: MicroK8s tracks upstream and releases beta, release candidate, and final bits the same day as upstream K8s. You can track the latest K8s or stick to any release version from 1.10 onward.
  • Comprehensive: MicroK8s includes a curated collection of manifests for common K8s capabilities and services (e.g., meshes, observability, ingress, auto updates, and GPGPU bindings).

In this lab, you will learn how to:

item.title: Minikube
item.description:
minikube is a method for creating a local, single-node Kubernetes cluster for development and testing. Setup is completely automated and doesn’t require a cloud provider account.

You are highly encouraged to install minikube on your local development machines such as a laptop.

In this lab, you will learn how to:

    ☐ Install minikube
    ☐ Configure minikube
    ☐ Differentiate the minikube CLI tool from the Kubernetes KubeCtl tool
    ☐ Inspect a cluster
    ☐ Use addons
